the underground burrow in which foxes shelter and raise cubs.

feeding by scooping sediment and sorting out microscopic organic matter (algae, bacteria, detritus) from mineral grains.
working out a roundabout path to prey in advance from visual information, then following it even when the prey is out of sight.

color vision based on two cone types; horses see mainly blues and yellows and are not colorblind.

the daily up-and-down movement of animals through the water column, typically rising to feed at night and descending by day.

the scientific name of the wandering albatross.
a marsupial with two prominent forward-pointing lower incisors, as in kangaroos.

leaving the birth community to join another; in bonobos, females disperse at adolescence.
the departure of a young tiger from its natal range to seek vacant territory of its own.
the departure of maturing dogs, often in same-sex sibling groups, to found or join new packs.
the departure of maturing young foxes from the natal territory to establish ranges of their own.
the movement of young birds away from where they were raised to establish territories of their own.

moving locally in response to conditions such as rainfall and food, rather than undertaking regular long-distance migration.
a stereotyped behavior used in communication, such as male peafowl fanning.
a stereotyped behavior used in communication.

feigned-injury or other behavior by which a female lures predators from her brood.
conspicuous behavior, such as feigning injury, used to lead a predator away from eggs or young.

active during daylight.
active during daylight.

the breaking of a pair bond between two living birds that then pair with others.
a covered nest with a side entrance, built by the female lyrebird, usually on or near the ground.
a population shaped by generations of selective breeding under human control, as distinct from its wild ancestor.

the process of selection for traits favorable in captivity, reshaping wild species.

a relationship in which one animal reliably yields to another over a resource; not the same as leadership.
a ranking of individuals; in wild wolf families this largely reflects the natural parent–offspring structure rather than combat among unrelated adults.
the ranked ordering of individuals that structures access to resources and mates.

a ranked social order that, once established through displays and recognition of individuals, reduces repeated conflict over resources.

a ranked social order that determines priority of access to resources such as safe burrows and mates.
a ranking of individuals by competitive status that determines priority of access to resources such as shelters and mates.
a ranking of individuals in a group based on aggressive and submissive interactions.
a ranking among individuals that influences access to resources such as food or nest sites.
The informal, situational ordering of access to a rich food source (such as a salmon run) by size, age, and experience.
a ranked social arrangement, especially among males, that governs priority of access to resources and mates and reduces continual conflict once established.
the breeding male and female whose reproduction the pack collectively supports.
the breeding female and male that monopolize most reproduction in a meerkat group.
the safety line a jumping spider trails behind it, anchoring leaps and enabling controlled falls; it is not used to catch prey.
A male honeybee, produced mainly in the reproductive season, whose role is to mate with queens from other colonies.
a rapid, rhythmic burst of blows on a resonant surface used as a long-distance signal, not for feeding.
rolling in dry soil or sand to clean feathers and control parasites; a natural comfort behavior.
working dry soil or dust through the plumage, thought to help maintain feather condition and control parasites.

rapid, adjustable concealment achieved by matching the color, brightness, and texture of the background in near real time.

sensing by sound: emitting clicks and interpreting the returning echoes to locate and characterise objects and prey.
sensing by sound: emitting clicks and interpreting the returning echoes to locate and characterise prey and surroundings.
the drab plumage adopted by drakes during the late-summer flightless moult, resembling the female.
an organism that reshapes its physical environment for many other species; termites engineer soil structure, decomposition, and nutrient cycling.
an organism that modifies habitat in ways that create resources for other species, as woodpeckers do by making cavities.
an organism that physically modifies habitat in ways that affect many other species, as elephants do by felling trees and digging waterholes.
an organism that physically modifies its habitat; lyrebirds move large quantities of litter and soil while foraging.
a population adapted to a particular habitat (e.g. inshore versus offshore) that differs behaviourally and genetically from others of its species.
a population adapted to a particular way of life (e.g. fish-eating resident versus mammal-eating Bigg's) that differs in diet, body form, dialect, and behaviour, and rarely interbreeds with others.
an animal that relies on external heat sources rather than internal metabolism to regulate body temperature.
an animal that relies on external heat sources to regulate body temperature, as all snakes do.
an animal that relies on external heat sources to regulate body temperature ("cold-blooded"), with generally low energy needs.
the evolution of parasite eggs that resemble host eggs, reducing the chance of rejection.

a host defence in which a foreign egg is recognised and removed or abandoned.

the innate act by which a cuckoo chick heaves host eggs and nestlings out of the nest.
a member of the family Elapidae, the front-fanged venomous snakes that includes cobras, kraits, mambas, coral snakes, and sea snakes.

an IUCN Red List category indicating a species faces a very high risk of extinction in the wild.
native to and found only within a particular, often small, geographic area.

any internally generated biological timer that continues to cycle in constant conditions; fiddler crabs have both circadian and tidal endogenous clocks.
wearing prey to exhaustion through prolonged pursuit, exploiting stamina rather than a single burst of speed or strength.
additions to an environment (rooting substrate, space, nest material, social stability) that allow natural behaviors and improve welfare.
Provision of stimulation, foraging challenges, and social contact designed to meet the psychological and social needs of captive animals.
the injection of venom by a bite; in humans a king cobra envenomation is a serious medical emergency.

the family of estrildid finches (waxbills and grassfinches), to which the zebra finch belongs.
the conspicuous anogenital swelling that signals a female's fertile phase.
the study of animal behavior, especially in natural conditions, of which Lorenz was a founder.

the highest level of social organisation, marked by overlapping generations, cooperative care of young, and a reproductive division of labour between reproducers and non-reproducing helpers.

the feathery, blood-rich gill structures on the sides of the head, used to extract oxygen directly from water.
mating outside the pair bond.
mating outside the social pair bond, resulting in offspring not sired by the social mate.
mating outside the social pair bond, resulting in offspring not sired by the social mate.
(plural **eyases**) — a nestling peregrine (or other falcon) still in the nest; a falconry term also used in ornithology.
a pattern of concentric rings resembling an eye on peafowl tail coverts.

the nesting ledge or site of a bird of prey such as the peregrine.
the ability to identify and remember specific individuals by their faces, shown strongly in sheep.
the ability to identify and remember specific individual human faces or forms.

a pair together with their goslings, which remain together through the first migration and winter.
the core social group of related females and their dependent offspring led by a matriarch.
the act of a sow giving birth to a litter of piglets.

an alliance of females that cooperate, especially to support one another and collectively counter male aggression.

to leave the nest, typically around the time of first flight.
